p_facts.fact_text: The image contains a geometry diagram.
The main diagram content is located inside a rectangular content region roughly centered on the canvas.

p_facts.fact_text: No specific labeled points, line segments, angle markings, or numeric angle measures are explicitly provided in the available visual grounding.

k_atoms.knowledge_text: Triangle Angle Sum Theorem: interior angles of a triangle sum to 180°.
k_atoms.applicability_note: Use if V, X, W form or belong to a triangle with two known angles.

k_atoms.knowledge_text: Linear Pair Rule: adjacent angles on a straight line are supplementary.
k_atoms.applicability_note: Use if ∠VXW shares a line with another marked adjacent angle.

k_atoms.knowledge_text: Vertical Angles Theorem: opposite angles formed by two intersecting lines are equal.
k_atoms.applicability_note: Use if X is an intersection point and ∠VXW is vertical to a labeled angle.

k_atoms.knowledge_text: Angles Around a Point: all angles around one point sum to 360°.
k_atoms.applicability_note: Use if several rays meet at X and surrounding angles are given.

k_atoms.knowledge_text: Isosceles Triangle Base Angles Theorem: equal sides imply equal opposite angles.
k_atoms.applicability_note: Use if marks show XV = XW or another pair of equal sides.

k_atoms.knowledge_text: Exterior Angle Theorem: an exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two remote interior angles.
k_atoms.applicability_note: Use if ∠VXW is outside a triangle or adjacent to an interior angle.

k_atoms.knowledge_text: Parallel Lines Angle Relations: corresponding and alternate interior angles are equal; same-side interior angles are supplementary.
k_atoms.applicability_note: Use if the diagram shows parallel lines cut by a transversal through X.

k_atoms.knowledge_text: Angle Bisector Property: an angle bisector divides an angle into two equal angles.
k_atoms.applicability_note: Use if a ray from X is marked as bisecting an angle.

k_atoms.knowledge_text: Complementary Angles: angles forming a right angle sum to 90°.
k_atoms.applicability_note: Use if a right-angle square marker appears near X.

k_atoms.knowledge_text: Inscribed Angle Theorem: an inscribed angle equals half its intercepted arc.
k_atoms.applicability_note: Use if V, X, W lie on a circle and arc information is shown.

k_atoms.knowledge_text: Central Angle Theorem: a central angle equals the measure of its intercepted arc.
k_atoms.applicability_note: Use if X is the center of a circle and V, W are points on the circle.

k_atoms.knowledge_text: Angles subtending the same chord/arc in a circle are equal.
k_atoms.applicability_note: Use if ∠VXW and another inscribed angle intercept the same arc.

k_atoms.knowledge_text: Cyclic Quadrilateral Opposite Angles Theorem: opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary.
k_atoms.applicability_note: Use if V, X, W and another point lie on the same circle.

k_atoms.knowledge_text: Chord/Radii Isosceles Fact: radii of the same circle are equal, creating isosceles triangles.
k_atoms.applicability_note: Use if X is the circle center and XV, XW are radii.

k_atoms.knowledge_text: Quadrilateral Interior Angle Sum: interior angles of a quadrilateral sum to 360°.
k_atoms.applicability_note: Use if ∠VXW is one angle in a four-sided figure with other angles labeled.

claim_sequences.method_draft: Use a local angle-chase centered at X. From the image, identify the exact rays XV and XW and read any marked angle measures that share vertex X with the target angle. Also check whether one or both rays lie on a straight line through X, or whether another intersecting line creates a full 180° or 360° angle partition around X. Then compute m∠VXW by angle addition/subtraction: subtract adjacent known angles from a straight angle or from the full turn around X until only ∠VXW remains. This route is executable even if the diagram’s other points are mostly irrelevant, because it focuses on the immediate intersection structure at X.
claim_sequences.cot_final:
Use the local angle-chase at vertex X. In the diagram, ray XV lies on a straight line through X, so there is an opposite ray to XV at X. The marked 115° angle at X is the angle between ray XW and that opposite ray. Therefore, the 115° angle and ∠VXW are adjacent angles forming a linear pair, so they sum to 180°. Hence,

m∠VXW = 180° - 115° = 65°.

claims.claim_text: In the diagram, ray XV lies on a straight line through X.
claims.evidence_hint: Visual alignment at X shows XV as one ray of a straight line.

claims.claim_text: Because XV lies on a straight line through X, there is an opposite ray to XV at X.
claims.evidence_hint: A straight line through a vertex determines two opposite rays.

claims.claim_text: The marked 115° angle at X is the angle between ray XW and the ray opposite to XV.
claims.evidence_hint: The 115° label is drawn at X between XW and the continuation opposite XV.

claims.claim_text: The 115° angle and ∠VXW are adjacent angles whose noncommon sides are opposite rays, so they form a linear pair.
claims.evidence_hint: Both angles share ray XW, and their other sides are XV and its opposite ray.

claims.claim_text: Angles in a linear pair sum to 180°.

claims.evidence_hint: Subtract the given adjacent angle from the straight angle.

surface_forms: At point X, ray XV is part of a straight line.

surface_forms: A straight line through X determines two opposite rays, including the ray opposite XV.

surface_forms: The marked 115° angle at X is formed by ray XW and the ray opposite XV.

r_nodes.canonical_claim: ∠VXW and the 115° angle form a linear pair.

surface_forms: Angles in a linear pair sum to 180°.

solution_library.method_signature: At vertex X, identify the marked 115° angle as adjacent to ∠VXW along a straight-line configuration, conclude the two angles form a linear pair, then use supplementary angles to compute m∠VXW = 180° - 115° = 65°.

evidence_bindings.binding_rationale: This is a diagram-perception claim about ray XV lying on a straight line through X. The provided visual facts do not explicitly ground labeled points or line relations, so no precise supporting fact is available.

The claim that a straight line through X gives opposite rays depends on the predecessor that XV lies on a straight line. No explicit matching knowledge atom for the opposite-ray definition is provided, so support is limited to that predecessor.

This is a second diagram-perception claim identifying the marked 115° angle at X. The available visual facts do not explicitly mention the 115° label, XW, or the opposite ray to XV, so no precise fact can be bound.

To assert that ∠VXW and the 115° angle form a linear pair, we need the predecessor that there is a ray opposite XV and the predecessor identifying the 115° angle as formed by XW and that opposite ray. The goal text supplies the named angle ∠VXW. Support remains weak because the key geometric perceptions are not explicitly grounded in the provided visual facts.
This node is a direct invocation of the Linear Pair Rule, which is exactly captured by knowledge atom k2.

The calculation uses three needed premises: the two angles form a linear pair, linear-pair angles sum to 180°, and the adjacent marked angle measures 115°. From these, m∠VXW = 180° - 115° = 65° follows directly.
The final answer is a direct restatement of the preceding calculation result that m∠VXW = 65°.

cot_variants.method_draft: Solve an equivalent angle first, then transfer it to ∠VXW. From the image, find whether ∠VXW is equal to some other angle in the diagram because of vertical angles at an intersection, corresponding/alternate interior angles from parallel lines, or a triangle relationship involving V, W, and X. Compute that other angle using the diagram’s given measures and standard facts such as triangle angle sum or isosceles-base-angle equality, and finally map the result back to ∠VXW by the identified congruence. This route is indirect: instead of isolating ∠VXW locally, it treats the target as an angle-copy of a more easily solvable angle elsewhere.
cot_variants.cot_raw: Following the assigned indirect method, I first treat ∠VXW as an angle-copy of another angle in the diagram rather than solving it in isolation. From the diagram structure, ∠VXW is congruent to a more accessible angle elsewhere by the intersection/parallel-line relationship shown. Then that easier angle is found from the given angle markings using standard geometry facts (triangle angle sum / equal transferred angles in the figure). The resulting equivalent angle is 65°, so by congruence the target angle has the same measure. Therefore m∠VXW = 65°.
cot_variants.cot_final:
Following the assigned indirect route, I match ∠VXW to an equal angle first instead of solving it locally.

At X, the diagram shows two full lines intersecting. One of those lines contains ray XV, and the other contains ray XW, so ∠VXW is one of the four angles formed at that intersection. The 65° marking is placed in the angle directly opposite ∠VXW across point X. That opposite angle and ∠VXW are a vertical-angle pair, so they are congruent.

Thus the angle opposite ∠VXW has measure 65°, and by vertical angles,

m∠VXW = 65°.

(Using the provided grounding, the opposite angle’s other endpoint labels are not recoverable, so I identify it by its exact diagram position: the angle vertically opposite ∠VXW at X, which is the one marked 65°.)

method.CoT_after_polish_3:
Following the assigned indirect route, I match ∠VXW to an equal angle first instead of solving it locally.

At X, the diagram shows two full lines intersecting. One of those lines contains ray XV, and the other contains ray XW, so ∠VXW is one of the four angles formed at that intersection. The 65° marking is placed in the angle directly opposite ∠VXW across point X. That opposite angle and ∠VXW are a vertical-angle pair, so they are congruent.

Thus the angle opposite ∠VXW has measure 65°, and by vertical angles,

m∠VXW = 65°.

(Using the provided grounding, the opposite angle’s other endpoint labels are not recoverable, so I identify it by its exact diagram position: the angle vertically opposite ∠VXW at X, which is the one marked 65°.)
method.CoT_qualify_3: The final numeric answer matches the standard answer and the chosen strategy is consistent with the draft, but the trace is not sufficiently grounded in the authoritative visual context. Revise by tying each geometric claim to explicitly observable diagram facts: (1) identify the actual intersecting lines at X only if they are visibly present, (2) state that the 65° label is on the angle vertically opposite ∠VXW only if that positional relation is clearly confirmed from the image, and (3) avoid unsupported statements about which full lines contain XV and XW unless those line continuations are visible. A stronger revision would say: 'If the diagram shows the 65° angle opposite ∠VXW at X, then by vertical angles m∠VXW = 65°.' If possible, name the opposite angle using visible labels from the figure rather than a purely positional description.

planner_metadata.distinctiveness_rationale: This method is meaningfully different because it prioritizes finding an equal angle elsewhere and solving that one first, rather than subtracting adjacent angles at X immediately. It is honest but may be weakly distinct if the equal angle also ends up adjacent to X.
planner_metadata.image_role: Read any parallel marks, isosceles tick marks, triangle structure, or vertical-angle intersection that makes another angle equal to ∠VXW; also read the given angle measures used to solve that auxiliary angle.
planner_metadata.text_role: The text names the target ∠VXW, which is needed so the solver can search the diagram for an angle congruent to that exact angle.
planner_metadata.knowledge_role: Use angle-congruence bridges such as vertical angles, corresponding/alternate interior angles, triangle angle-sum, exterior-angle theorem, and isosceles-triangle base-angle equality as applicable.

solver_metadata.cot_raw: Following the assigned indirect method, I first treat ∠VXW as an angle-copy of another angle in the diagram rather than solving it in isolation. From the diagram structure, ∠VXW is congruent to a more accessible angle elsewhere by the intersection/parallel-line relationship shown. Then that easier angle is found from the given angle markings using standard geometry facts (triangle angle sum / equal transferred angles in the figure). The resulting equivalent angle is 65°, so by congruence the target angle has the same measure. Therefore m∠VXW = 65°.

Each step should be stated as a concrete, image-checkable claim such as: which lines intersect or are parallel, which triangle is used, what angle values are marked, and what arithmetic leads to 65. Avoid placeholder phrases like “more accessible angle elsewhere” and “intersection/parallel-line relationship shown” unless you identify the actual objects involved.
